passages.explanation: Jesus tells his disciples not to be troubled. His Father's house has many rooms — he's going ahead to prepare a place for them and will come back to bring them there. He says they know where he's going and the way to get there. He's reassuring them: his departure isn't abandonment, it's preparation.

Thomas says what the others are probably thinking: they don't know where Jesus is going, so how can they know the way? Jesus responds with one of his most direct identity statements: 'I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.' If they had truly known him, they would have known the Father. From now on, he says, they do know the Father and have seen him.

Philip asks Jesus to just show them the Father and they'll be satisfied. Jesus sounds frustrated: he's been with them all this time and Philip still doesn't know him? Seeing Jesus is seeing the Father. The words he speaks aren't his own — the Father living in him does the works. He tells them to believe on the basis of his words, or at least on the basis of the works themselves. Then he makes a striking promise: anyone who believes in him will do the same works he does — and even greater ones — because he's going to the Father. Whatever they ask in his name, he will do it.

Jesus connects love to obedience: if they love him, they'll keep his commandments. He promises to ask the Father to send another Counsellor — the Spirit of truth — who will be with them forever. The world can't receive this Spirit because it doesn't recognise him. But the disciples know him because he already lives with them and will be in them. Jesus says he won't leave them as orphans. The world will stop seeing him, but they will see him. Because he lives, they will live. Whoever has his commandments and keeps them is the one who loves him, and the Father will love that person, and Jesus will reveal himself to them.

Judas (a different Judas, not Iscariot) asks why Jesus is going to reveal himself to the disciples but not to the world. Jesus' answer reframes the question: anyone who loves him will keep his word, and the Father will love them, and both the Father and Son will come and make their home with that person. Those who don't love him won't keep his words. The revelation isn't exclusive by design — it's exclusive because only those who respond with love and obedience will experience it.

Jesus says he's told them these things while still physically present. The Holy Spirit, sent by the Father in Jesus' name, will teach them everything and remind them of everything Jesus said. He leaves them with peace — not the kind the world gives, but his own peace. He tells them again not to be troubled or afraid. If they loved him, they'd be glad he's going to the Father, because the Father is greater than he is. He's telling them now so that when it all happens, they'll believe. He won't speak much more with them — the 'prince of this world' is coming, but has no hold on him. Everything Jesus does is to show the world he loves the Father and obeys him. He tells them to get up and go.
